text.title: Giving and Receiving Feedback Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding feedback. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Feedback message MUST contain all three SBI fields: Situation (when/where), Behavior (observable), Impact (consequence). Missing any field is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Without Situation it's vague; without Behavior it's personality attack; without Impact the recipient cannot judge importance.

rule.statement: Behavior field MUST be observable (verb + object) not interpretive ('you were disrespectful' is forbidden — 'you interrupted twice during demo' is required).
rule.rationale: Observable behavior is debate-able with facts; interpretation is not.

rule.statement: When the behavior caused negative impact, the message MUST add Intent inquiry: 'What was happening for you?' This converts SBI into SBII.
rule.rationale: Skipping intent inquiry produces fundamental attribution error: assuming intent that wasn't there.

rule.statement: Author MUST tag intended Radical Candor quadrant: Radical Candor / Ruinous Empathy / Obnoxious Aggression / Manipulative Insincerity. Self-deception (saying 'Radical Candor' while sending Obnoxious Aggression) is the most common failure.
rule.rationale: Self-honest quadrant tagging surfaces miscalibration before delivery; the recipient feels the actual quadrant regardless of label.

rule.statement: When receiving feedback, the response MUST follow LEARN: Listen, Empathize, Acknowledge, Respond (after pause), Next steps.

rationale.source: Sheila Heen & Douglas Stone — Thanks for the Feedback

rule.rationale: Defensive 'but actually...' responses kill the feedback loop; LEARN keeps it open.
